rulings.Case Title: ASIAN AMERICANS ADVANCING JUSTICE ASIAN LAW CAUCUS ET AL VS. CITY AND COUNTY OF SAN FRACNISCO ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion For Peremptory Writ Of Mandate
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Tuesday, August 27, 2019, Line 12, PETITIONER ASIAN AMERICANS ADVANCING JUSTICE ASIAN LAW CAUCUS, COUNCIL ON AMERICAN-ISLAMIC RELATIONS, AMERICAN CIVIL LIBERTIES UNION OF NORTHERN's Motion For Peremptory Writ Of Mandate. Petitioners Asian Americans Advancing Justice - Asian Law Caucus, Council on American-Islamic Relations, California, and American Civil Liberties Union of Northern California's motion for peremptory writ of mandate is denied. Petitioners seek to compel disclosure of a white paper authored by the Federal Bureau of Investigation (FBI) which "provides clarification and understanding" of the Joint Terrorism Task Force-San Francisco Police Department Memorandum of Understanding, the City's 2012 Safe Streets City Ordinance, and SFPD's General Order 8.10, "Guidelines for First Amendment Activities." The motion is denied because the white paper is property of the federal government and Respondents are prohibited by federal law from disclosing it. Petitioners' remedy is to seek its disclosure directly from the FBI under the federal Freedom of Information Act. The Court's full tentative ruling is emailed to the parties. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for Respondents is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: ANDRIY KRAVCHUK VS. CITY AND COUNTY OF SAN FRANCISCO ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Motion For A New Trial
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Tuesday, August 27, 2019, Line 2, PLAINTIFF ARTEM KOSHKALDA's Motion For A New Trial. Plaintiff Artem Koshkalda's motion for a new trial is denied. The court lacks jurisdiction to rule on the motion, which is untimely. (Code Civ. Proc., §659(a)(2) [notice of intent to move for new trial must be filed within 15 days after service of written notice of entry judgment]; Kabran v. Sharp Memorial Hospital (2017) 2 Cal.5th 330, 337 ["the trial court loses jurisdiction to hear a new trial motion if no notice of intent is filed within 15 days of the mailing or service of notice of entry of judgment"].) Judgment was entered on June 12, 2019 and notice of entry of judgment was served on plaintiff by electronic mail on June 19, 2019, but the motion was not filed until August 2, 2019. The court's July 30, 2019 order correcting a minor typographical error in its original judgment (regarding the date on which the City's memorandum of costs was filed) did not change the effect of the judgment or extend the time to file a motion for new trial. The court also notes that it does not appear that plaintiff has standing to pursue the matter. The court takes judicial notice that plaintiff filed a petition for relief under the Bankruptcy Code and scheduled the instant lawsuit as an asset of the bankruptcy estate, and that E. Lynn Schoenmann was appointed as plaintiff's Chapter 7 Trustee. Under the Bankruptcy Code, the Trustee administers property of the bankruptcy estate, including causes of action. (11 US.C. § 704(a); Haley v. Dow Lewis Motors, Inc. (1999) 72 Cal.App.4th 497, 503-504 ["'It is of course indisputable that any causes of action which accrue to a debtor who has filed for relief under the Bankruptcy Act before the filing of the bankruptcy petition become the property of the bankruptcy estate and may thereafter be prosecuted only by the trustee or a duly appointed representative of the estate.'"].) Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for the City is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested.=(302/JPT)

Delfino (2005) 35 Cal.4th 180, 190 ["an appeal from the denial of a motion to compel arbitration automatically stays all further trial court proceedings on the merits."].) =(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: STANFORD-STARTX FUND, LLC ET AL VS. MEDWHAT.COM INC. ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion For Sanctions Against All Plaintiffs And Stanford University Pursuant To Code Of Civil Procedure Sections 128.5 And 128.7
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Tuesday, August 27, 2019, Line 11, DEFENDANT ARTURO DEVESA's Motion For Sanctions Against All Plaintiffs And Stanford University Pursuant To Code Of Civil Procedure Sections 128.5 And 128.7. Defendant Devesa's motion for sanctions is denied. This motion largely repeats defendant's procedurally improper motion to strike. The motion for sanctions is denied because defendant failed to comply with the safe harbor provision that allows a party to withdraw an offending pleading. (Code Civ. Proc., § 128.5(f)(1)(B).) In addition, the motion fails on the merits. Defendant fails to make an evidentiary showing that plaintiff's complaint is a frivolous pleading that completely lacks merit or was presented for a harassing purpose. Plaintiff's request for sanctions is denied. rulings.Case Title: STANFORD-STARTX FUND, LLC ET AL VS. MEDWHAT.COM INC. ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: MOTION TO STRIKE COMPLAINT
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Tuesday, August 27, 2019, Line 11, DEFENDANT ARTURO DEVESA's MOTION TO STRIKE COMPLAINT. Defendant Devesa's motion to strike is denied. First, the motion is untimely because defendant already answered the complaint. (See City and County of San Francisco v. Strahlendorf (1992) 7 Cal.App.4th 1911, 1913 ["A motion to strike must be filed 'within the time allowed to respond to a pleading ....' (§ 435, subd. (b)(1).)"].) Second, the grounds for the motion to strike must appear on the face of the pleading or on matters the court can judicially notice. (Id.) Defendant must bring a motion for summary judgment if he wishes to contest the factual basis of plaintiff's allegations. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If the tentative ruling adversely affects, in whole or in part, any party who is not represented by an attorney, then any other party (whether represented by an attorney or not) may submit on the tentative ruling without appearing for the hearing only if the adversely affected self-represented party has stated in writing or orally that it, he or she will not appear at the hearing. Counsel for plaintiffs is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portions of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested.=(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: ELLIOT TAYLOR ET AL VS. MICHAEL CALLAHAN ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: DEMURRER TO 1ST AMENDED CROSS COMPLAINT
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Tuesday, August 27, 2019, Line 5, CROSS DEFENDANT ELLIOT TAYLOR, RON TAYLOR, 2441-2449 2ND STREET LLC's DEMURRER TO 1ST AMENDED CROSS COMPLAINT. Cross-defendants Elliot Taylor, Ron Taylor and 2441-2449 2nd Street LLC's demurrer to the first amended cross-complaint is sustained with 20 days leave to amend. Cross-complainant has leave to add Michael Callahan, Greener Future, LLC, NAPA PAL, and ST5 Holdings, LLC as indispensable parties and to plead derivative claims on behalf of those entities. "Where the plaintiff seeks some type of affirmative relief which, if granted, would injure or affect the interest of a third party not joined, that party is in indispensable party." (Sierra Club, Inc. v. California Coastal Comm'n (1979) 95 Cal.App.3d 495, 501; see Olszewski v. Scriopps Health (2003) 30 Cal.4th 798, 8908 [person is indispensable party "when the judgment to be rendered necessarily must affect his rights"]; see also Deltakeeper v. Oakdale Irrig. Dist. (2001) 94 Cal.App.4th 1092, 1106 [ordinarily, all parties to a conrtact are necessary parties in an action involving rights under the contract].) The court rejects movant's remaining arguments. The amended cross-complaint does not disclose on its face that the MOU was terminated. That is a factual issue. Even if the statute of frauds applies, it does not bar the claims in their entirety. The fraud allegations contain sufficient specificity. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for the cross-defendants is required to prepare a proposed order which states that the motion was unopposed and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested.=(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: DAJERVU TORREY VS. MV PUBLIC TRANSPORTATION, INC. ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion To Compel Further Responses To Its Form Interrogatories General Set One And Form Interrogatories Employment Law, Set One And Request For Monetary Sanctions In The Amount Of $7,300.75
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Tuesday, August 27, 2019, Line 4, DEFENDANT MV TRANSPORTATION, INC.'s Motion To Compel Further Responses To Its Form Interrogatories General Set One And Form Interrogatories Employment Law, Set One And Request For Monetary Sanctions In The Amount Of $7,300.75. Pro Tem Judge Chuck Geerhart, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: Granted in part. Plaintiff shall provide within 14 days of service of this order verified responses (objection-free except as to privilege or work product) to interrogatories 2.2, 2.5, 2.6, 2.7, 2.8, 6.7, 8.6, and 8.7; and 203.1, 205.1, 208.1, 210.2, and 212.2. Motion denied as to 12.6, 200.4, and 207.2. Sanctions denied. First, defendant did not prevail on all aspects of the motion. Second, the Court perceives this discovery dispute to be largely caused by plaintiff's counsel's failures to comply with discovery. However, the notice fails to state the names of persons against whom sanctions are sought as required by CCP sec. 2023.040 ("A request for a sanction shall, in the notice of motion, identify every person, party, and attorney against whom the sanction is sought..."). As there are at least two lawyers who have been involved in this case for plaintiff, the notice is defective in failing to identify which lawyer(s) it seeks sanctions against. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to cgeerhart@gmail.com with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: DAJERVU TORREY VS. MV PUBLIC TRANSPORTATION, INC. ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion To Compel Further Responses To Its Request For Production Of Documents, Set One And Request For Monetary Sanctions In The Amount Of $5,800.75
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Tuesday, August 27, 2019, Line 4, DEFENDANT MV TRANSPORTATION, INC.'s Motion To Compel Further Responses To Its Request For Production Of Documents, Set One And Request For Monetary Sanctions In The Amount Of $5,800.75 (Part 1 of 2). Pro Tem Judge Chuck Geerhart, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: Motion granted in part. As to the demand for production of documents, Plaintiff shall provide verified responses (objection-free except as to privilege or work product) and documents within 14 days of service of this order as follows: RPD Nos. 6, 7, 8, 22, 30, 31, 35, 36, 56, 65, 67, 68, 69, 72, 73, 74, 75, 76, 83, 84, and 94. If privilege is claimed, plaintiff shall produce a privilege log identifying the name of the document, type of document, date created, preparer, recipient, general subject matter and legal reason for withholding. Motion denied as to RPD 15, 24, 25, 79, 80, 85, 86, 87, 88, and 92 (statement that after diligent search, no documents exist is sufficient), and 18-19 (under right to privacy, discovery into plaintiff's entire medical history without limitation as to specific physical or mental condition(s) that has been placed in issue is not permitted under Britt v. Superior Court (1978) 20 Cal.3d 844, 864. "Mere speculation as to the possibility that some portion of the records might be relevant to some substantive issue does not suffice." (Davis v. Superior Ct. (1992) 7 Cal.App.4th 1008, 1018.) The party seeking access to constitutionally protected information has the burden of proving the direct relevance of the information sought. (Id. at 1017.) ) (End of Part 1 - Continued to Part 2.) =(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: ROBERT STANG ET AL VS. BRY 1890, LLC ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion For Terminating Sanctions For Discovery Misuse
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Tuesday, August 27, 2019, Line 7, DEFENDANT BRY1890 LLC,, MRY101 LLC's Motion For Terminating Sanctions For Discovery Misuse. The report and recommendation of the judge pro tem is adopted in full. Defendants and Cross-Complainants Bry1890 LLC and MRY101 LLC's motion for terminating sanctions for discovery misuse is granted. As the report recites, over a period of more than 9 months, plaintiffs Robert Stang and Luigi Di Ruocco failed to respond to discovery requests properly served on them, ignored defendants' repeated communications requesting that they comply, failed to oppose six motions to compel, and then disregarded orders of the court granting those motions and requiring them to serve discovery responses and pay monetary sanctions. When plaintiffs finally did provide some written responses, just days before the hearing on defendants' motion, their responses were woefully deficient. Plaintiffs did not even bother to file a memorandum in opposition to defendants' motion for terminating sanctions, but instead filed only a brief declaration of counsel which offered absolutely no explanation for plaintiffs' failure to comply with their discovery obligations or even to respond to communications from opposing counsel, and instead improperly accused plaintiffs-without foundation-of propounding voluminous discovery that "seemed largely propounded primarily to exert the maximum burden on Defendants." Plaintiffs' unfounded speculation about defendants' motive for seeking discovery cannot by any stretch of the imagination justify their repeated failures to comply with their obligations under the Discovery Act and with the court's orders. Under the circumstances, the court agrees that plaintiffs' repeated failures to provide timely discovery responses and to comply with the court's orders constitute willful, repeated, and egregious misuse of the discovery process that warrant the imposition of terminating sanctions. (See, e.g., CREED-21 v. City of Wildomar (2017) 18 Cal.App.5th 690, 701-703; Department of Forestry and Fire Protection v. Howell (2017) 18 Cal.App.5th 154, 197-198; Los Defensores, Inc. v. Gomez (2014) 223 Cal.App.4th 377, 390-392.) The recommended sanctions are less harsh than those sought by defendants. The court finds that no less severe sanction would be effective to produce full compliance with the discovery rules or to ensure that defendants receive a fair trial. Accordingly, plaintiffs' complaint filed October 19, 2018 is dismissed with prejudice. Within 30 days of the date of this order, plaintiffs shall file code-compliant discovery responses, without objection, to all of defendants' discovery requests except those specifically pertaining to plaintiffs' complaint. Finally, within 30 days of the date of this order, plaintiffs and their counsel shall pay all sanctions previously awarded against them, in the amount of $2,400, and shall further pay to defendants the additional sum of $2,655, representing the reasonable attorney's fees incurred by defendants in bringing the motion. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for defendants is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested.=(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: ANDREW RUITTER VS. LYFT, INC. ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: DEMURRER TO 1ST AMENDED COMPLAINT
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Tuesday, August 27, 2019, Line 8, DEFENDANT LYFT, INC.'s DEMURRER TO 1ST AMENDED COMPLAINT. Defendant Lyft, Inc.'s demurrer to Plaintiff Andrew Ruiter's first amended complaint is overruled. Joinder is proper here because there is reasonable uncertainty as to the extent of each Defendant's liability for Plaintiff's injuries. (See Code Civ. Proc. § 379(c) ["Where the plaintiff is in doubt as to the person from whom he or she is entitled to redress, he or she may join two or more defendants, with the intent that the question as to which, if any, of the defendants is liable, and to what extent, may be determined between the parties."].) The first amended complaint pleads with sufficient particularity the injury, the negligence by successive tortfeasors, and doubt as to the extent of liability of each of the Defendants for his injuries. (See Landau v. Salam (1971) 4 Cal.3d 901, 905-908 [reversing trial court's order sustaining demurrer without leave to amend where complaint properly pled doubt as to liability for plaintiff's injuries and properly joined defendants as successive tortfeasors in separate motor vehicle collision and premises liability incidents that occurred over three months apart].) Specifically, the first amended complaint pleads injuries to Plaintiff's head, neck, and upper extremities from the first accident-involving Lyft driver Defendant Leonard Rodrigues De Souza-and that the second accident-involving Defendants Sing Chim Shiu and Mai Xue Fang-caused new injuries and may have aggravated the injuries to Plaintiff's neck and upper extremities. (FAC 15, 19-20, 23-25.) Lyft's objection that joinder will cause it additional expense and burden is misplaced. (See Landau v. Salam, 4 Cal.3d at 908 ["We recognize that if we permit a single trial of the two causes, some parties will encounter some inconvenience. But the minor travail must succumb to the larger loss; the bifurcation of the litigation would entail the greater expenditure of time and energy."].) The cases relied upon by Lyft are distinguishable. Jensen v. Home Depot, Inc. (2018) 24 Cal.App.5th 92, 94 did not involve intertwined injuries and the need to allocate fault and liability, but rather the misjoinder of two different plaintiffs whose claims did not arise out of the same transaction or raise common issues of law or fact. Likewise, Hoag v. Superior Court (1962) 207 Cal.App.2d 611, 617-620 involved four entirely different transactions and the court concluded that the plaintiff in that case was seeking joinder for the ulterior motive of forum shopping. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Plaintiff is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested.=(302/JPT)
